coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots in-situ relieve humic acids inhibition on methanogens while significantly increasing the abundance of Methanosarcinaceae
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qiang
Xu, Yuanting
Li, Yeqing
Ma, Chengjie
Chen, Shuo
Feng, Lu
Xu, Quan
Pan, Junting
Peng, Bo
Zhou, Hongjun
Xu, Chunming
coredata.dc:description: Monophenols form humic acids (HA) through polycondensation reaction in the anaerobic digestion (AD) process, which will inhibit AD process. Currently, metal ions are the option for in-situ relieving HA inhibition during AD, but excess metal ions are harmful to microorganisms. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs, a non-metallic materials) were proposed to relieve HA inhibition in-situ. We investigated the effect of HA on AD acidification and methanation stage, and synthesized CQDs using sodium citrate (s-CQDs) and p-phenylenediamine (p-CQDs) as precursors to relieve the HA inhibition in-situ. Results showed that s-CQDs (3.0 g/L) significantly increased the cumulative CH4 yield from AD of ethanol with 1.0 g/L HA (1.9 times higher than that without s-CQDs). Microbiological analysis indicated the most dominant methanogen was Methanosarcinaceae, with richness of 89.7%. Compared to the HA inhibition system, the relative abundance of Methanosarcinaceae increased by 87.5%. The analysis of interaction mechanism between CQDs and HA indicated that s-CQDs has an in-situ binding effect to HA by reacting with -OH, CC, and -COOH. This study provided a novel means for in-situ relieving HA inhibition, and illustrated the interaction mechanism between CQDs and HA, which will guide the application in production of bioenergy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Humic acids
Anaerobic digestion
Microbial community
Interaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CH4 yield corresponding to HA and s-CQDs/p-CQDs additive
Microbial community composition and dynamics in digesters
Mechanism of interaction between CQDs and HA

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors appreciate the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22278435 ), the Strategic Cooperation Technology Projects of CNPC and CUPB ( ZLZX2020-04 ), China Finland joint international funding by NSFC (No. 52211530034 ), the Agricultural Science and Technology Innovation Program of Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences , Fundamental Research Funds for Central Non-profit Scientific Institution (No. 1610132020003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the synthesis of s-CQDs (blue) and p-CQDs (bluish violet) via one-step hydrothermal method. (b) Particle size distribution and (c) TEM diagram of s-CQDs. (d) Particle size distribution and (e) TEM diagram of p-CQDs. (f) C 1s XP and (g) O 1s XP of s-CQD. (h) C 1s XP and (i) O 1s XP of p-CQD. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Effect of HA content on cumulative CH4 yield in (a) ethanol and (b) acetic acid AD system. Effects of s-CQDs and p-CQDs dosages on the cumulative CH4 yield of (c) ethanol and (d) acetic acid AD system with 1.0 g/L HA. Effects of s-CQDs and p-CQDs dosages on the daily CH4 yield of (e) ethanol and (f) acetic acid AD system with 1.0 g/L HA.

(a) The VFA composition in AD (substrate: ethanol) with different HA contents (0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1.0 g/L) at different time interval. (b) TVFA concentration on the day 7 in the AD system with different HA contents. (c) The acetic acid concentration in AD (substrate: ethanol) with different HA contents at different time interval.

(a) Relative abundance of the bacteria at the level of Family in the ethanol AD system. Others <0.02%. “Control”: Group without HA; “HA”: Group with 1.0 g/L HA; “HA s-CQDs”: Group with 1.0 g/L HA and 3.0 g/L s-CQDs; “HA p-CQDs”: Group with 1.0 g/L HA and 1.0 g/L p-CQDs. (b) Relative abundance of the archaea at the level of Family in the ethanol AD system. Others <0.02%. Note: “HA p-CQDs” was the group with 1.0 g/L HA and 3.0 g/L p-CQDs. (c) Associations between methanogens and bacteria with average relative abundance >0.1% among all substrate samples were assessed via network analysis. The colour of the nodes represents different orders, and the edges represent the relationship between the species. (p value < 0.05, Spearman's coefficient ≥0.5). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) FT-IR spectrum of s-CQDs and p-CQDs. (b) Changes in HA functional groups in the AD system after adding s-CQD or p-CQD. (c) Changes in HA functional groups after mixing HA with s-CQD. (d) C 1s XP of HA in the AD system with HA and s-CQD or (e) p-CQD. (f) C 1s XP of HA after mixed with s-CQDs. (g) Schematic diagram of CQDs combined with HA to relieve HA inhibition of microorganisms.
(a) Content of HA oxygen-containing functional groups before and after AD. (b) Content of HA oxygen-containing functional groups after AD with 3.0 g/L s-CQDs or p-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of HA before and after AD. (b) C 1s XP of HA (b) before and (c) after AD.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots in-situ relieve humic acids inhibition on methanogens while significantly increasing the abundance of Methanosarcinaceae

State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, Beijing Key Laboratory of Biogas Upgrading Utilization, College of New Energy and Materials, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
Beijing Key Laboratory of Biogas Upgrading Utilization
College of New Energy and Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monophenols form humic acids (HA) through polycondensation reaction in the anaerobic digestion (AD) process, which will inhibit AD process. Currently, metal ions are the option for in-situ relieving HA inhibition during AD, but excess metal ions are harmful to microorganisms. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs, a non-metallic materials) were proposed to relieve HA inhibition in-situ. We investigated the effect of HA on AD acidification and methanation stage, and synthesized CQDs using sodium citrate (s-CQDs) and p-phenylenediamine (p-CQDs) as precursors to relieve the HA inhibition in-situ. Results showed that s-CQDs (3.0 g/L) significantly increased the cumulative CH4 yield from AD of ethanol with 1.0 g/L HA (1.9 times higher than that without s-CQDs). Microbiological analysis indicated the most dominant methanogen was Methanosarcinaceae, with richness of 89.7%. Compared to the HA inhibition system, the relative abundance of Methanosarcinaceae increased by 87.5%. The analysis of interaction mechanism between CQDs and HA indicated that s-CQDs has an in-situ binding effect to HA by reacting with -OH, CC, and -COOH. This study provided a novel means for in-situ relieving HA inhibition, and illustrated the interaction mechanism between CQDs and HA, which will guide the application in production of bioenergy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, biomass has become one of the most promising resources for renewable energy. In Europe, bioenergy accounts for more than 50% of the total renewable energy, which is an important means to achieve the goals to reduce GHG emission (Scarlat et al., 2019). Despite China's total amount of biomass reaching 5 billion tce, the bioenergy only accounts for 0.1% of primary energy consumption (Yang et al., 2010). Therefore, it is extremely important to develop and utilize these abundant biomass resources for achieving emission reduction and optimizing energy structures (Geissler and Maravelias, 2022).

Anaerobic digestion (AD) is an important technology for creating bioenergy, which utilizes microorganisms to convert organic substances into biogas (CO2 and CH4) and digestate through hydrolysis, acidification, and methanogenesis (Wang et al., 2015). However, in the AD process, substrates (such as lignocellulosic) could be degraded into monophenols and further form humic acids (HA) through polycondensation [5]. HA is a complex macromolecule with a particle size of approximately 500 nm. It contains carboxylic acid, phenolic, and quinone functional groups (Ait Baddi et al., 2009). Although HA can advance the degradation of macromolecular substrates into micromolecules as an electron shuttle in AD system, it can also compete with methanogens for electrons (Nie et al., 2018). In addition, research showed that the active functional groups of HA were able to combine with the active site of microorganisms’ key enzymes to inactivate the enzyme, which would inhibit the transformation efficiency (J. Li et al., 2019).

Currently, the methods to relieve HA inhibition in-situ are limited, which including addition of hydrolase or metal ions (Fernandes et al., 2014). However, the hydrolase was costly and cannot relieve the inhibition in methanation stage. Metal cations were able to combine with HA through electrostatic interaction and ion exchange to form hydroxyl complex — M(OH)n. Therefore, they were effective material for relieving inhibition in-situ (Li et al., 2021a). Increasing the metal ions dosage led to HA flocculation; however, an excess of metal ions would negatively impact microorganisms (Salehi and Madaeni, 2010). Another study showed that dual metal ions added at the low concentrations could reach the synergistic effect of 1 + 1>2, but the optimal relieving efficiency still cannot reach up to 100% (Li et al., 2021a). Therefore, the inhibition of HA cannot be completely relieved on the premise of avoiding metal ions harming microorganisms. Carbon materials, particularly carbon-based nanomaterials, possess biocompatibility and specific functions that are preferable to metal ions. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as nanomaterials (<10 nm), possess not only biocompatibility but also photoluminescence, excellent conductivity and interfacial activity (bQ. Li et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2017; Hu et al., 2019; Mintz et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018). The characteristics of high interfacial activity and photoluminescence make it often used for component detection. For example, Liu et al., prepared CQDs with excellent selectivity for Fe3+, and the detection limit of 1.14 μM (Liu et al., 2020). Additionally, in vitro cytotoxicity tests showed that CQDs were nontoxic. The high interfacial activity and low cytotoxicity give it the potential to replace metal ions. At present, there are no reports on the use of CQDs to relieve HA inhibition in AD. Therefore, it is worth to study the in-situ binding of CQDs with HA to relieve HA inhibition. This method was expected to break the limitation of using metal ions to relieve HA inhibition in-situ. Considering the economic and environmental benefits of AD in treating waste organics, the application of CQDs in this field is conducive to waste biomass treatment and bioenergy recovery.

In this study, s-CQDs and p-CQDs were synthesized with sodium citrate and p-phenylenediamine as precursors, respectively, using hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 (a)). In the AD system of different substrates with 1.0 g/L HA, s-CQDs proved to be more effective in in-situ relieving HA inhibition, and the cumulative CH4 yield was significantly increased compared with the inhibition system. In addition, high dosages of CQDs were not harmful to microorganisms. Moreover, the introduction of s-CQDs increased the abundance of methanogens, and the abundance of Methanosarcinaceae raised from 2.2% to 89.7%. The in-situ combining of CQDs and HA is the internal mechanism of the relieving inhibition, and the oxygen-containing functional groups and aromatic functional groups are the key factors in the combination of CQDs and HA.

Sodium citrate (AR, 98%) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd, (Shanghai, China). p-Phenylenediamine (AR, 97%) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Tec Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Humic acids (FA ≥ 90.0%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd., (Shanghai, China). Inoculum utilized in this study was collected from the Asuwei Landfill (Beijing, China). Prior to start, 0.5 g/L glucose was added and acclimated at 37 °C for 1 month. A batch of inoculum will be domesticated before each batch tests. Therefore, the inoculum properties of each batch test are different, and the properties need to be determined before the test, including total solid (TS) and volatile solid (VS). Deionized (DI) water with a resistivity of >15 MΩ cm was obtained from a Milli-Q Water Purification System and used in all experiment.

The CQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method (Fig. 1) (Vercelli et al., 2021): To prepare the CQDs, 1.43 g of sodium citrate and 0.35 g of zinc chloride were dissolved in 50 mL of DI water under ultrasound. The mixture was sonicated, transferred to a Teflon autoclave, and heated at 185 °C for 4 h. After cooling the autoclave naturally to 25 °C, the remaining supernatant was filtered using 0.22 μm hydrophilic filters and dialyzed against DI water in a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500 Da) for 24 h. The filtering procedure was repeated three times to ensure the effective removal of precursor residues and minor derivatives. The purified sodium citrate-CQDs (s-CQDs) powder was obtained from supernatant after drying at 80 °C. For the p-CQD samples, p-phenylenediamine was used as the carbon source instead of sodium citrate.

Batch AD experiments were conducted in 250 mL serum bottles under mesophilic conditions (37 °C) with magnetic stirring, which had a working volume of 100 mL. The initial substrate (ethanol or acetic acid) concentration was 5.0 g/L, and the organic matter ratio of the substrate and inoculum was 1:2. The dosage of the s-CQDs (or p-CQDs) was 1.0 g/L (or 3.0 g/L). All experimental groups were in triplicate.

Methane production was determined using Methane Potential Test System (AMPTS-II system, Bioprocess Control, Sweden). The anaerobic fermentation bottles were placed in a water bath to keep constant temperature during the entire period. Biogas was purified by passing through the absorption bottle containing NaOH to remove CO2 and H2S. After purification, the generated CH4 was recorded by a gas meter.

HA was extracted in accordance with a modified method described by the International Humic Substances Society (IHSS) (Thurman and Malcolm, 1981). Activated sludge (1.0 g) from wastewater treatment plant was stirred for 24 h in 0.5 M NaOH (10 mL) at room temperature (25 °C) under N2 atmosphere. Then the suspension was subsequently centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm. The solution was filtered to remove insoluble materials and acidified to pH 2 with 6 M HCl. After overnight standing at 4 °C, the mixture was centrifuged for 10 min to separate the HA and supernatant. The entire procedure was repeated a second time. HA was then redissolved in N2 atmosphere with 0.1 M KOH and KCl ([K+] = 0.3 M). Subsequently, suspended solids were removed by centrifugation, and acidified to pH 1 with 0.6 M HCl. After overnight standing at 4 °C, the HA extract was demineralized by stirring for 16 h at room temperature under N2 atmosphere in HCl and HF solutions of 0.1 and 0.3 M, respectively. Finally, the HA were washed with deionized water, freeze-dried, and labeled as AS-HA.

The carboxyl and phenolic hydroxyl contents in HA were identified by potentiometric titration (METTLER TOLEDO, T70, Switzerland). Firstly, the sample was dissolved in 5 M NaOH, and then the pH was adjusted to 3 using a nitric acid solution as the starting point of the titration. The volumetric standard NaOH was used as the titrant. Finally, the content of the carboxyl group was equal to the variation in NaOH from pH 3 to 8, and the content of the phenolic hydroxyl group was twice that of NaOH from pH 8 to 10. The total acidity was the sum of the phenolic hydroxyl and carboxyl content (Ma et al., 2001).

TS and VS were determined in according with the standard method of the American Public Health Association (Gilcreas, 1966). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer, with an Al-K α Single X-ray source excited (1486.6 eV). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was obtained using a Bruker Vertex 70V spectrometer. The surface configuration of the CQDs was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (model JEM-2100). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of the samples were measured using a UV–Vis spectrometer (Jasco V-570). One-way ANOVA analysis of variance was performed using SPSS (IBM, NY 10504, USA).

The modified Gompertz mathematical equation was used to evaluate AD system (Li et al., 2020; Donoso-Bravo et al., 2010):
(1)
B =
B 0
× exp
{
− exp
[
e ×
μ m
×
(
λ − t
)
B 0
+ 1
]
}
Where, B is the cumulative CH4 yield for the digestion time t (mL/g VS), B
0 is ultimate CH4 yield of the feed-stock (mL/g VS), μ
m is the maximum yield rate (mL/g VS/day), λ is the lag time (days), t is the digestion time (days), and e is a natural constant.

The fermentation substrate (10 mL) was taken from the reactor at the end of AD and stored at - 20 °C. Microbial DNA was extracted from the samples using a Soil DNA Kit (Omega Bio-tek, Norcross, GA, USA). Thereafter, the primer set F338 (5′-ACTCCTACGGGAGGCAGCAG-3′)/R806 (5′-GGACTACHVGGGTWTC-TAAT-3′) was used for bacteria, and the primer set 524F10extF (5′-TGYCAGCCGCCGCGGTAA-3′)/Arch958RmodR (5′-YCCGGCGTT-GAVTCCAATT-3′) was used for archaea to the V3-V4 hypervariable regions (Guo et al., 2020). The results showed that the size of the target band and the concentration of PCR products were correct, and subsequent experiments could be conducted.

Fig. 1(a) showed that CQDs synthesized with different carbon sources had varying fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet radiation. The average particle size of the s-CQDs and p-CQDs was 6.04 ± 0.15 nm and 4.15 ± 0.09 nm, respectively (Fig. 1(b)–(e)). The precursor has significant influence on the particle size. The XPS survey of the s-CQDs and p-CQDs presented peaks corresponding to C and O. The C and O content from s-CQDs was determined to be 55.4% and 44.6%, respectively, corresponding to the O/C of 0.81; while that from p-CQDs was 86.6% and 13.4%, and the O/C was 0.17 (Table S1). C 1s XP spectrum (Fig. 1(f)) of s-CQDs showed binding energies of 284.6 eV, 285.6 eV, and 288.1 eV for three components, i.e., CC, C-O, and CO, respectively. O 1s XP spectrum (Fig. 1(g)) showed two sub-peaks at 531.1 eV and 532.0 eV, which can be assigned to CO and C-O. p-CQDs possessed the same functional groups as s-CQDs (Fig. 1(h) and (i)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CH4 yield corresponding to HA and s-CQDs/p-CQDs additive
ce:section.ce:para: The HA inhibition in AD occurred during the methanogenic stage. As showed in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), the cumulative CH4 yield in AD system with ethanol and acetic acid gradually decreased as HA content increased. In the ethanol AD, the cumulative CH4 yield was lower than 200 mg/L after the addition of HA. And the cumulative CH4 yield in ethanol AD with 1.0 g/L HA decreased by 43.5% compared to without HA (213.15 ± 0.00 mL/g VS). Similarly, the inhibition rate was 51.05% in acetic acid AD. Additionally, the modified Gompertz dynamic model (R2 = 0.9376–0.9933) demonstrated that the existence of HA significantly increased the lag time (λ) (Table S2). Hence, the potential inhibition due to HA on AD cannot be ignored. The potential reason for this inhibition could be due to the combination of HA and the active site of the microorganisms’ key enzyme in the digesters, which could result in enzyme inactivation (Wang et al., 2022).

It is noteworthy that the cumulative CH4 yield exceeded the uninhibited system after adding s-CQDs or p-CQDs in the inhibited system. Especially for ethanol AD, the cumulative CH4 yield increased by 164% after adding 3.0 g/L s-CQDs. The cause for this phenomenon was that CQDs not only relieved HA inhibition in-situ, but also promoted methanogenesis. Li et al. proposed that CQDs can be used as capacitors to transmit and receive electricity, thus promoting the potential direct interspecies electricity transfer (DIET) and metabolism between microorganisms (Li et al., 2020).

In this study, an ethanol AD system with different concentrations of HA was established to explore the impact on acidification. The volatile fatty acids (VFA) were mainly consisted of acetic acid and butyric acid on day 1 (Fig. 3 (a) and Fig. S1), which was further switched to acetic acid and propionic acid. On day 2, the acetic acid concentration gradually increased from 56% to 63% with the increased HA addition. Isobutyric acid was firstly observed at day 5 and the total VFAs (TVFA) peaked at day 7. With 1.0 g/L HA addition, the TVFA concentration reached to 6413 mg/L and that was 20.7% higher than without HA (Fig. 3(b)). This result was consistent with Li et al. (2020). Fig. 3(c) showed the daily variations of acetic acid and propionic acid concentrations corresponding to different HA addition. When the TVFA concentration reached the peak value (the day 7), the acetic acid concentration was 2801 mg/L, 2912 mg/L, 3075 mg/L, and 3,130 mg/L, respectively. Compared with that in AD without HA, the concentration of acetic acid increased by 6.3%, 10.5%, 16.6%, and 18.8%, respectively. Similarly, the concentration of propionic acid increased to 843.1 mg/L, 958.4 mg/L, 976.4 mg/L, 1535.0 mg/L, and 1464.6 mg/L, with an increase of 13.7%, 15.8%, 82.1%, and 73.7%, respectively (Fig. S2). These results showed that HA acts as an exogenous additive accelerated the acidification process. HA contains quinone, nitrogen, and sulfur functional groups that can accelerate the electron transfer between microorganisms to enhance acidification (Yuan et al., 2017). The mechanism was that acidification bacteria oxidized organic substances in cells and then released electrons. The electrons were then transferred to the extracellular acceptor through the electron transfer chain (ETC) (Thapa et al., 2022), while HA acts as an ETC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial community composition and dynamics in digesters
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the effect of adding HA and/or CQDs on the microbial community, we determined the relative abundances of bacteria (Fig. 4 (a)) and archaea (Fig. 4 (b)) in the residue of the ethanol AD system. Fig. 4(a) showed that the bacterial communities in the control group were mainly composed of Clostridiaceae, Peptostreptococcaceae and Erysipelotrichaceae, with respective abundances of 63.3%, 15.8%, and 8.7%. In the group with 1.0 g/L HA (named “HA”), the relative abundances of Clostridiaceae, Peptostreptococcaceae, Erysipelotrichaceae and Corynebacterium were 58.8%, 8.7%, 9.8%, and 6.9%, respectively. Due to HA inhibition, the relative abundance of Clostrididae declined by 4.6%. After adding 1.0 g/L p-CQDs in “HA” (named “HA p-CQDs”), the relative abundances of Clostridiaceae, Peptostreptococcaceae, and Erysipelotrichaceae were 61.7%, 14.2%, and 9.6%, respectively. Compared with that in “HA”, the relative abundance of Clostridium increased by 2.9%. In the “HA” group with 3.0 g/L s-CQDs (named “HA s-CQDs”), the bacterial community was mainly composed of Clostridiaceae, Peptostreptococcaceae, Erysipelotrichaceae, and Limnochordia, with respective abundances of 64.5%, 16.0%, 6.4%, and 6.1%. The relative abundance of Clostridiaceae was 5.8% higher than that in “HA”. In addition, compared with that in group without HA (named “Control”), the relative abundance of Clostridium increased by 1.2%. Clostridium, belongs to the Firmicutes, can metabolize various organic substances (Ju et al., 2017). Research has shown that it can hydrolyze cellulose, proteins, and polysaccharides to produce VFA, and further oxidize VFA such as acetic acid (Schink, 1984). Therefore, the upregulation of the relative abundance of Clostrididae was one of the factors contributing to the improvement in methanogenesis efficiency.

Fig. 4(b) showed the relative abundance of archaea after the addition of and/or CQDs. In “Control”, the archaea community primarily comprised Methanogenicmicrobe (50.4%), Methanosarcinaceae (30.8%), and Methanobacteriae (5.6%), with a total abundance was 86.8%. The HA inhibition did not induce the change of species types of archaea, but its abundance changed into 67.5%, 2.2%, and 12.0%, respectively, and the total abundance decreased to 81.6%. Notably, 1.0 g/L HA significantly reduced the relative abundance of Methanosarcinaceae by 28.6%, and the total abundance of methanogens by 5.7%. At this time, the cumulative CH4 yield decreased significantly (Fig. 2(a)). Therefore, HA mainly inhibited the methanogenesis by reducing the relative abundance of Methanosarcinaceae. In “HA p-CQDs”, the archaea community was mainly comprised of Methanemicrobiaceae (22.3%), Methanosarcinaceae (17.5%), and Methanobacteriaceae (51.3%), with a total abundance of 91.2%. Compared to “HA”, the relative abundance of Methanosarcinaceae increased by 15.3%, indicating that p-CQDs relieved the HA inhibition in-situ to a certain extent. After adding 3.0 g/L s-CQDs, the relative abundance of Methanosarcinaceae reached a striking 89.7%. Compared to “HA”, the relative abundance of Methanosarcinaceae increased by 87.5%, while the total abundance of methanogens increased by 12.6%. In addition, compared to “Control”, the relative abundance of Methanosarcinaceae increased by 39.3%, and the total abundance of methanogens increased by 7.44%.

One of the main modes for HA to inhibit microorganisms is contact with microorganism to reduce enzymatic activity. The significant increase in Methanosarcinaceae was primarily attributed to the in-situ binding of s-CQDs with HA, which prevented direct contact between HA and Methanosarcinaceae. And this was the main reason for in-situ relieving HA inhibition. Furthermore, the complex formed by s-CQDs combined with HA still possessed abundant functional groups and excellent conductivity, thereby promoting direct interspecies electron transfer (DIET). Research has showed that Methanosarcinaceae were able to produce methane through DIET (Khoei et al., 2021). In this suitable environment established through the interaction between s-CQDs and inhibitors, Methanosarcinaceae gradually enriched to a higher abundance.

Co-occurrence network based on correlation (Fig. 4(c)) illustrated the relationship between different microorganisms in various niches (Mercado et al., 2022). The size of each node was proportional to the number of connections (i.e., degree) and the edge thickness (weight) of each connection between two nodes was proportional to the correlation coefficient. For the bacteria and archaea, the absolutely dominant populations were Clostridiaceae (5 nodes), Peptostreptococcaceae (4 nodes), Methanobacteriaceae (6 nodes), and Methanosarcinaceae (4 nodes), respectively. The results showed that Clostridiaceae possessed extensive niche, which depended on the metabolic ability to utilize a range of organic substrates. In addition, previous studies have indicated that Clostridaceae can be used as a signal for communication with other species (Torcato et al., 2019). For example, it has been positively correlated with Methanosarcinaceae. The fact that Clostridaceae and Metanosarcinaceae had more edges were able to indicate this communication. Therefore, Clostridaceae could share the same niche with methanogens, further driving Clostridaceae to produce multiple functions and establish a wide range of signals, thus facilitating energy-sharing in the AD process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of interaction between CQDs and HA
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between substances can transform the functional groups (Lian et al., 2020). FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 5 (a)) showed that p-CQDs mainly contained N-H (3300 cm−1), C-H (2950 cm−1), CO (1599 cm−1 and 1388 cm−1), CC (1599 cm−1), and C-O (1260 cm−1), while the s-CQDs mainly contained O-H (3515 cm−1), C-H (2850 cm−1), CC (1577 cm−1), CO (1376 cm−1), and C-O (1200 cm−1). The functional groups of HA after AD mainly included O-H (3411 cm−1), C-H (2849 cm−1), CC/CO (1580 cm−1), and C-N (1457 cm−1) (Fig. 5(b)). In addition, the main functional groups of HA after AD with HA and s-CQDs were O-H (3420 cm−1), C-H (2850 cm−1), CC/CO (1600 cm−1), and C-O (1005 cm−1). The existence of s-CQDs resulted in significant differences in the HA functional groups, particularly in the hydroxyl, carboxyl, and aromatic functional groups. This suggested a strong interaction between s-CQDs and HA, which enabled s-CQDs to relieve inhibition in-situ. In addition, the spectrum of s-CQDs-HA showed a red-shift, which was attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds during the binding process between s-CQDs and HA. Due to the formation of hydrogen bonding groups, the absorption frequency decreases, but at the same time, the absorption intensity increases, which led to the shift of the absorption band towards the low wavenumber direction (Chen et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2019). The interaction between s-CQDs and HA was speculated to occur primarily through -OH, CC, and -COOH bonds. Previous research has shown that the interaction between HA and carbon-based materials depends on both space repulsion and electrostatic interaction (Hyung and Kim, 2008). Furthermore, the addition of p-CQDs did not impact the HA functional groups, which indicated a weak interaction between HA and p-CQDs. To further demonstrate the interaction between HA and s-CQDs, we mixed s-CQDs with HA before extracting it and then analyzed the resulting structure. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the hydroxyl, carboxyl, and aromatic functional groups were significantly more pronounced after mixing HA and s-CQDs, which confirmed a strong interaction between them.

The structures were analyzed further using XPS (Fig. 5(d) and (e); Tables S4 and S5). The CC proportion in HA after AD with HA and s-CQDs (or p-CQDs) was 32.4% (or 28.3%), that of C-C was 41.4% (or 49.3%), that of C-O was 14.7% (or 11.7%), and that of CO was 11.5% (or 10.4%) respectively. After mixed with s-CQDs, the proportion of CC decreased to 26.1%, that of C-C decreased to 30.5%, that of C-O decreased to 32.0%, and that of CO remained relatively unchanged at 11.4% (Fig. 5(f) and Table S6). The diminish in CC and CO further proved that HA and s-CQDs had a strong interaction through the aromatic functional groups, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups. This combination was beneficial for relieving HA inhibition, as it prevented HA from binding to microorganisms (Fig. 5(g)).

Variety in the contents of HA oxygen-containing functional groups (phenolic hydroxyl, carboxyl, and total acidity) before and after AD were showed in Fig. 6 (a). The contents of HA phenolic hydroxyl, carboxyl and total acidity before AD were 2.4 mmoL/g, 6.0 mmoL/g, and 8.4 mmoL/g, respectively, while the corresponding values after AD after AD were 3.2 mmoL/g, 6.7 mmoL/g, and 9.9 mmoL/g, respectively. After AD, the HA phenolic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups increased by 0.8 mmoL/g and 0.7 mmoL/g, respectively, leading to a total acidity increase of 1.5 mmoL/g. Consequently, the aromaticity and humification degree of HA improved. AD also resulted in a significant reduction in the aliphatic fraction of HA and an increase in its aromaticity (Fig. 7 (a)). Compared to the aromatic functional group, the aliphatic group of HA had a simpler and more linear structure, making it more susceptible to degradation during AD. Upon exposure to the AD process, the aliphatic functional groups of HA were more easily oxidized, accompanied with the enrichment of aromatic components (Fig. 7(a)). Therefore, the total acidity of HA showed an increasing trend after AD. The changes in HA phenolic hydroxyl, carboxyl, and total acidity before and after AD with CQDs are showed in Fig. 6(b). Upon adding 3.0 g/L s-CQDs, the contents of HA phenolic hydroxyl, carboxyl and total acidity were 2.8 mmoL/g, 6.2 mmoL/g, and 9.0 mmoL/g, respectively, while upon adding 3.0 g/L p-CQDs, the corresponding values were 3.0 mmoL/g, 6.5 mmoL/g, and 9.5 mmoL/g, respectively. In the 3.0 g/L s-CQDs and p-CQDs systems, the contents of HA phenolic hydroxyl decreased by 0.5 mmoL/g and 0.2 mmoL/g, and that of the carboxyl group decreased by 0.5 mmoL/g and 0.2 mmoL/g as well, leading to a decrease in total acidity of 0.9 mmoL/g and 0.4 mmoL/g, respectively. The 3.0 g/L s-CQD system exhibited a significant decrease in the oxygen-containing functional groups contents, indicating an interaction between s-CQDs and HA. This finding was consistent with the previous conclusion.

After AD, the contents of the HA amide functional groups (C-N, 1457 cm−1) and aliphatic functional groups decreased, accompanying with aromatic functional groups increasing (Fig. 7(a)). The amide functional groups in HA could be used as a carbon source for microorganisms to produce CH4 and micromolecules, which were degraded first (Gao et al., 2019). Subsequently, the micromolecules were linked to the HA via the polymerization process, and finally formed a structure with a high content of oxygen-containing functional groups and a high degree of humification (Tang et al., 2018). Moreover, the elemental composition and chemical bonds of HA before and after AD were analyzed (Fig. 7(b) and (c)). Before AD, the C-C content of HA was 33.5%, followed by CC and O-C at 11.3% and 7.6%, respectively, while the CO content was the lowest at 20.2%. After AD, the CO content of HA was increased significantly, reaching 39.1%, followed by the C-C and finally C-O, which were 33.7% and 18.2%, respectively, while the CC decreased to 9.1%. The elemental contents of HA before and after AD were listed in Table S7. The whole proportions of C in HA before and after AD were 78.02% and 50.6%, respectively. The overall proportions of O were 21.98% and 49.06%, respectively. The O/C ratios of HA before and after AD were 0.28 and 0.98, respectively. The content of oxygen-containing functional groups in HA after AD increased. After AD, the functional groups of HA interacted with organic substance to form a compact spherical structure. Such structure increased the content of HA oxygen-containing functional groups and humification degree and further restricted the conversion of organic components (Xu et al., 2020).

Compared to HA after AD without CQDs, the addition of p-CQDs decreased the SUVA254 and SUVA280 of HA by 0.680 and 0.691, while the addition of s-CQDs decreased them by 0.790 and 0.612, respectively (Table S8). Compared to p-CQDs, the values at SUVA254 and SUVA280 of the HA after adding s-CQDs decreased by 0.11 and 0.08, while E2/E3 and E4/E6 increased by 0.08 and 3.21, which indicated lower humification degree. The functional groups of s-CQDs may have interacted with the functional groups of HA, leading to a reduced ability of HA to form compounds with macromolecules and a decrease in the degree of humification.

HA promoted acidification in the AD system since it accelerated the electron transfer, with a promotion efficiency of 17.3%. However, the HA inhibition on methanation led to a decrease of 43.5%–51.1% in cumulative CH4 yield. The addition of s-CQDs and p-CQDs was able to relieve the HA inhibition on microorganisms in-situ, especially the s-CQDs synthesized with sodium citrate. The results showed that 3.0 g/L s-CQDs promoted the cumulative CH4 yield of ethanol AD with 1.0 g/L HA to 350.0 ± 5.0 mL/g VS, which was 1.9 times higher than without s-CQDs. Microbiological analysis indicated that 3.0 g/L s-CQDs significantly promoted the abundance of dominant methanogens, especially the relative abundance of Methanosarcinaceae, which reached 89.7%. Compared to the HA inhibition system, the relative abundance of Methanosarcinaceae increased by 87.5%. In addition, high-abundance methanogens established extensive signals with Clostridiaceae to further improve energy sharing in the AD system. Functional groups analysis revealed that s- CQDs and HA were mainly bound through -OH, CC, and -COOH; this interaction led to s-CQDs relieving inhibition in-situ. These findings highlighted the potential of CQDs as an effective means of relieving HA inhibition in-situ and provided insights into the interaction mechanism between CQDs and HA, which would guide their use in bioenergy development and utilization. Meanwhile, it should be pointed out that the high cost and low yield of synthesized CQDs limited their large-scale applications. The green and efficient synthesis of CQD using biomass will be the focus in the future research.

Qiang Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yuanting Xu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yeqing Li: Conceptualization, Project administration, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Chengjie Ma: Data curation, Formal analysis. Shuo Chen: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Lu Feng: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Quan Xu: Conceptualization, Project administration, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Junting Pan: Conceptualization, Project administration, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Bo Peng: Project administration, Investigation, Methodology. Hongjun Zhou: Project administration, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Chunming Xu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhou et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Integrating biolayer interferometry, atomic force microscopy, and density functional theory calculation Studies on the affinity between humic acid fractions and graphene oxide
Environ. Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhou, Q., Ouyang, S., Ao, Z., Sun, J., Liu, G., Hu, X., 2019. Integrating biolayer interferometry, atomic force microscopy, and density functional theory calculation Studies on the affinity between humic acid fractions and graphene oxide. Environ. Sci. Technol. 53(7), 3773-3781.
